message.content: Hey — pulling up a support ticket that came in this morning. #4471 from Maya R. She says: 'I clicked Delete Account on my settings page last month and got billed again this month. What happened?' She's frustrated. Pulling up her user record now.

content.text:
Pulling up ticket #4471. The billing-after-deletion pattern is a classic race condition — user record was hard-deleted but the Stripe subscription wasn't cancelled before the next billing cycle fired.

Let me look at the deletion handler to confirm.

Right — this is two compounding failures.

**Failure 1: Irreversibility.** The action fires on a single click with no confirmation gate. Anyone who fat-fingers or muscle-memory-clicks the button is immediately and permanently deleted. No recovery path.

**Failure 2: Billing entanglement.** `db.user.delete()` cascades on the local DB but does nothing to Stripe. The subscription is still live, so the next billing webhook fires, looks up the customer ID, gets null from our DB, and either 404s or charges against a phantom record.

For failure 1, the right fix is **two layers**:

**Layer A — Soft-delete with 30-day grace.** Account-level delete is a soft-delete: set `deletedAt` to the request timestamp, sign the user out, send confirmation email, and let the nightly purge job hard-delete after the 30-day grace window. Never call `db.user.delete()` from a request handler. The 30-day window means accidental clicks are recoverable; Maya could have come back within a month to restore her account. We're calling this `dec-soft-delete-30day-grace-2026-02-18` — it applies any time a user-initiated or admin-initiated action removes a user, organization, workspace, or any account-shaped record.

**Layer B — Email-link confirmation, not just modal.** Destructive account-level actions require email-link confirmation, not in-app modal alone. The button sends the link; the action only proceeds when the user clicks the link in their email within 24 hours. After 24 hours the request expires silently with no state change. We're calling this `dec-confirm-via-email-link-2026-02-18` — it applies any time a UI action will terminate, anonymize, or irrevocably remove access to a user, organization, workspace, or paid resource.

The key point on Layer B: an in-app `confirm()` dialog or even a modal is still a single-session interaction. Maya scrolled past it as muscle memory. Email-link confirmation requires a context switch — open email, click link — which is exactly what breaks the muscle-memory failure mode. This is what the Feb-12 ticket is really about: the button was too easy to trigger.

For failure 2 (Stripe), let's look at that separately — it deserves its own constraint because the fix touches the Stripe SDK and there are subtleties around chargebacks.

message.content:
Both layer A and layer B make sense. Let's name them formally: dec-soft-delete-30day-grace and dec-confirm-via-email-link. Those are the two decisions from this session.

Now the Stripe side — how do we fix that?

content.text:
For Stripe, the failure is that we called `db.user.delete()` and nothing else. We need to think about what 'deleting a user' means for our billing state.

The naive fix is to call `stripe.customers.del(customerId)` when we delete the user. But that's actually wrong for two reasons:

1. **Deleting a Stripe customer wipes their payment history.** Stripe's customer object carries charge history, dispute records, and refund trails. We need these for chargeback response. If a user disputes a charge after account deletion, we need that history.

2. **The subscription still needs to be cancelled first.** Deleting the customer without cancelling the subscription leaves Stripe in an inconsistent state. The subscription cancellation needs `invoice_now: false, prorate: false` — we don't want a final invoice and we don't want a prorated refund triggered automatically.

The right approach for the Stripe side is a separate constraint we'll address in the follow-up implementation session. Let me sketch it now so it's captured.

message.content:
Got it — Stripe archive constraint is a separate follow-up. Let's close out the main decisions for today. We have dec-soft-delete-30day-grace-2026-02-18 and dec-confirm-via-email-link-2026-02-18 captured. I'll start the PR for the soft-delete migration.

What about the GDPR/CCPA implications? We've had a backlog of deletion requests from the GDPR and CCPA windows. Does the 30-day grace window create any compliance issues?

content.text:
The 30-day grace window is actually more compliant than immediate hard-delete for GDPR/CCPA, not less.

Both regulations require that users can request deletion AND that you honor that request within a reasonable window (GDPR: 30 days; CCPA: 45 days). The grace window satisfies both — as long as the actual purge runs before day 30/45. The key thing GDPR/CCPA care about is that PII is eventually gone from your production systems; they don't require that it happen in the same HTTP request as the user's click.

The grace window also helps you respond to retraction requests — if a user changes their mind within 24 hours and contacts support, you can restore the account without any data loss. That's user-friendly and doesn't conflict with either regulation.

One thing to watch: GDPR Article 17 says deletion requests must be honored 'without undue delay.' 30 days is explicitly within GDPR's own stated window. Your legal counsel should confirm this for your specific business, but the pattern is standard practice.

The CCPA and GDPR backlog you mentioned is actually a good test case for the new flow — all those pending deletion requests can go through the soft-delete path when the PR lands. You get the compliance credit without the accidental-click risk.

message.content:
That's reassuring. Okay, we have the two decisions. Let me write them up as substrate entries before we close this session.

1. dec-soft-delete-30day-grace-2026-02-18: Account-level delete is a soft-delete: set deletedAt to the request timestamp, sign the user out, send confirmation email, and let the nightly purge job hard-delete after the 30-day grace window. Never call db.user.delete() from a request handler. Applies when: a user-initiated or admin-initiated action removes a user, organization, workspace, or any account-shaped record.

2. dec-confirm-via-email-link-2026-02-18: Destructive account-level actions require email-link confirmation, not in-app modal alone. The modal sends the link; the action only proceeds when the user clicks the link in their email within 24 hours. After 24 hours the request expires silently with no state change. Applies when: a UI action will terminate, anonymize, or irrevocably remove access to a user, organization, workspace, or paid resource.

Both captured.

content.text:
Both captured correctly. The applies_when conditions are right — 'account-shaped record' is intentionally broad (it should catch workspace deletion, org deletion, team deletion, not just user deletion). The email-link condition uses 'irrevocably remove access' rather than just 'delete' — that's the right scope because some actions (like revoking an admin's role permanently) are just as dangerous as deletion.

One implementation note for the PR: the `DangerActionButton` component should route based on a `confirmation` prop — `'email-link' | 'modal'`. The default for new danger buttons should be `'email-link'`; a modal-only variant exists for lower-stakes actions like 'remove a card' where an in-app confirmation is sufficient. Don't hard-code the email-link path into the button — make it a prop so the confirmation level is visible at the call site.

Also: the nightly purge job needs a test. The soft-delete migration should include a test that confirms `deletedAt + 30 days < now()` records are hard-deleted on the next purge run. Without the test, someone will bump the grace window to 90 days for 'one customer' and forget there was a reason it was 30.

message.content: Back at it. Stripe paged us — webhook failure in prod. A billing renewal just fired for Maya's account and the handler threw a 500. Our webhook logs show: 'User not found for Stripe event customer.subscription.updated, customer id cus_Maya123.' The user record was deleted as expected (the soft-delete PR landed yesterday), but somehow the subscription is still active on Stripe's side and it tried to charge her.

content.text:
The PR landed but it only handled the local DB side — the soft-delete sets `deletedAt` and signs the user out, but nothing in the deletion flow touched Stripe at all. The subscription is still fully active. When the next billing cycle fired, Stripe sent the webhook, our handler looked up the user by customer ID, got null, and threw.

Let me check the webhook handler and the deletion flow side by side.

Got it. Two problems to solve:

**Problem 1:** The deletion flow never cancels the Stripe subscription or archives the customer. We need to add that step.

**Problem 2:** The webhook handler assumes the user always exists, which will keep throwing for deleted users whose subscriptions haven't been cleaned up yet.

For Problem 1, here's the constraint we need to capture: When deleting an account that has a `stripeCustomerId`, call `stripe.customers.update(id, { metadata: { archived: 'true', archived_at: <iso> } })`. Never call `stripe.customers.del()`. Subscriptions must be canceled first via `subscriptions.cancel()` with `invoice_now: false`, `prorate: false`.

We're calling this `con-stripe-customer-archive-2026-02-22`. It applies any time an account being purged or deactivated has a Stripe customer attached.

Why `update` instead of `del`? Stripe's `customers.del()` destroys the customer object including payment history, charge history, and dispute evidence. We need those for chargebacks. If a user disputes a charge made while their account was active, we need the payment history to respond. Archive with metadata gives us both: no future billing (subscription cancelled), full history accessible (customer object preserved).

For Problem 2, the webhook handler should be made null-safe — if the user is not found, check whether the customer has `metadata.archived = 'true'`. If archived, the webhook is expected; log and return 200. If not archived, that's a real error and should alert.

message.content:
That makes sense. Let me capture `con-stripe-customer-archive-2026-02-22` formally:

When deleting an account that has a stripeCustomerId, call `stripe.customers.update(id, { metadata: { archived: 'true', archived_at: <iso> } })`. Never call `stripe.customers.del()`. Subscriptions must be canceled first via `subscriptions.cancel()` with `invoice_now: false`, `prorate: false`.

Applies when: an account being purged or deactivated has a Stripe customer attached.

Now — how do I sequence the Stripe calls within `beginAccountDeletion`? What comes first, subscription cancel or customer archive?

content.text:
Subscription cancel first, then customer archive. The reason: you can't meaningfully 'archive' a customer who still has an active subscription — Stripe will continue billing until the subscription is cancelled regardless of metadata. The sequence should be:

1. Cancel all active subscriptions: `stripe.subscriptions.cancel(subId, { invoice_now: false, prorate: false })`
2. Archive the customer: `stripe.customers.update(customerId, { metadata: { archived: 'true', archived_at: new Date().toISOString() } })`

Use `invoice_now: false` so Stripe doesn't generate a final invoice on cancellation — you don't want a charge at the moment of deletion. Use `prorate: false` for the same reason — no prorated refund calculation triggered.

If the user has multiple subscriptions (enterprise accounts sometimes do), cancel all of them before archiving.

Also worth noting: you should wrap all of this in a try/catch and decide whether a Stripe failure should block the deletion or just log-and-continue. My recommendation: log-and-continue for the Stripe step, but emit an alert. Deletion should not be blocked by a Stripe API outage — the local soft-delete is the source of truth for your system. The Stripe cleanup is important but secondary.

message.content: Agreed on log-and-continue for Stripe. I'll add a Slack alert to the billing channel on failure. Constraint captured. The webhook handler fix is a separate PR — I'll make it null-safe for archived customers.

content.text:
Good. One more thing to add to `beginAccountDeletion` before we close this session: the ordering of all steps matters for failure modes. Here's the sequence I'd recommend for the full deletion flow:

1. Fetch the user record (you need `stripeCustomerId`, `email`, `id` for subsequent steps)
2. Set `deletedAt` on the user record (soft-delete — this is your source of truth)
3. Anonymize tax-held records (invoices, payments) — before hard-delete risk window
4. Cancel Stripe subscriptions + archive Stripe customer
5. Suppress mailing list
6. Send deletion confirmation email
7. Sign user out / invalidate sessions

Steps 3-5 are the 'coordination layer' — external systems that need to be notified. Step 3 is local DB, steps 4-5 are external APIs. All three should be wrapped in try/catch with log-and-continue semantics.

We haven't captured the mailing list constraint yet — that's coming from a different thread. But it belongs in this same function.

message.content: Finance just pinged. We got a response from the IRS regarding the audit they opened last month. The auditor is asking for invoices from November, December, and January for a specific account — it's Maya's account. The problem: those invoices don't exist in our system anymore. They were cascade-deleted when her user record was hard-deleted in January (before the soft-delete PR landed).

content.text:
This is the 'cascade delete tax records' failure mode. When `db.user.delete()` ran with no cascade overrides, Prisma's default behavior was to cascade-delete everything with a foreign key pointing to the user record — including Invoice records.

Let me check the Prisma schema to confirm what cascade rules were in place.

message.content:
Here's the relevant part:
```prisma
model Invoice {
  id        String   @id
  userId    String
  amount    Int
  createdAt DateTime
  user      User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
}
```

Yep — `onDelete: Cascade`. So when Maya's user record was hard-deleted, all her invoices went with it. We cannot produce them for the IRS.

Finance has confirmed: anonymize-in-place is legally sufficient. The auditor needs the financial data, not the PII. We can tell them the names are replaced with '[deleted user]' as part of our data retention policy — that's standard practice.

Can you write up the formal constraint text for con-anonymize-not-delete-tax-held-2026-03-04?

content.text:
Here's the formal constraint text:

**con-anonymize-not-delete-tax-held-2026-03-04**

Records subject to tax-record retention (invoices, payments, refunds, tax filings) must be anonymized, not deleted, when their associated user is purged. User-identifying fields (name, email, billing address) are overwritten with `[deleted user]`; the financial data and timestamps remain intact.

Applies when: an account purge would otherwise cascade-delete a record that legal/finance has flagged as held-for-retention (current list: Invoice, Payment, Refund, TaxFiling, OrgInvoice — see `lib/retention/held.ts`).

Justification: March-3 finding from the IRS audit response: the Feb deletion had cascade-deleted three months of the customer's invoices. We could not produce them; the auditor noted it as a control deficiency. The fix is anonymize-in-place; finance signed off on the `[deleted user]` placeholder as legally sufficient.

This constraint fires in `beginAccountDeletion` before the 30-day purge runs. The anonymize step happens during the soft-delete initiation — at the moment the user clicks Delete Account — so the PII is removed promptly (GDPR compliance) while the financial structure is preserved (tax retention). The Prisma schema change from `onDelete: Cascade` to `onDelete: SetNull` on Invoice, Payment, Refund, TaxFiling, and OrgInvoice is the schema guard.

message.content: Perfect. Constraint captured. Now I need to create lib/retention/held.ts with the list of retention-sensitive models, and update the Prisma schema. I'll also need to write a migration to add the userName and userEmail columns to Invoice (currently only userId is stored as a FK — PII is fetched from the User join, not stored directly on Invoice).

content.text:
Correct — if Invoice doesn't currently have denormalized `userName`/`userEmail` columns, the anonymize step needs those columns added first. The migration sequence is:

1. Add `userName String?` and `userEmail String?` to Invoice (nullable — backfill from User join)
2. Backfill existing invoices: `UPDATE Invoice SET userName = User.name, userEmail = User.email FROM User WHERE Invoice.userId = User.id`
3. Deploy the anonymize step in `beginAccountDeletion`
4. Change `onDelete: Cascade` → `onDelete: SetNull` on Invoice

Same sequence for Payment, Refund, TaxFiling, OrgInvoice — add denormalized PII columns, backfill, then change the cascade rule.

For `lib/retention/held.ts`, the pattern is a simple export:
```ts
export const RETENTION_HELD_MODELS = [
  'Invoice',
  'Payment',
  'Refund',
  'TaxFiling',
  'OrgInvoice',
] as const;
```

This list should be the source of truth for both the anonymize step and any future audit tooling. If a new retention-sensitive model is added, it goes here first.

message.content: Good sequencing. I'll open the migration PR today. One question before closing this session: should the anonymize step run when soft-delete is initiated, or only when the 30-day purge actually hard-deletes the record?

content.text:
Run the anonymize step when soft-delete is initiated — at the moment the user clicks Delete Account — not at the 30-day purge.

Reasoning: GDPR Article 17 says you should act on deletion requests 'without undue delay.' If you wait 30 days to anonymize PII, you've kept personal data for a month after the user requested deletion. Running anonymize immediately means the PII is gone within the same HTTP request window as the user's click. The financial structure (amounts, dates, IDs) is what you're retaining for tax purposes — that has no GDPR constraint.

Practically: the 30-day purge only hard-deletes the user record (after the grace window). Everything else — anonymize, Stripe archive, mailing list suppress — happens at request time. The 30-day window is specifically for the 'you can change your mind' recovery path; the external coordination (PII removal, billing teardown) should happen eagerly.

message.content: New issue. Just got a message from sales ops: they ran a CSV import into Mailchimp yesterday to re-engage lapsed subscribers. One of the emails in the CSV was Maya's. She got a newsletter this morning, saw our company name, and replied with a very unhappy message. Then forwarded it to the FTC's CAN-SPAM enforcement contact. We have a corrective-action request coming.

content.text:
This is the mailing list memory failure. Maya was 'deleted' from your system but there was no suppression record in Mailchimp. When the CSV import ran, Mailchimp had no memory of her having been unsubscribed — so it accepted the import and sent her the newsletter.

The fix is not to 'delete' the Mailchimp member on account deletion — deleting removes the record entirely, which means future imports can re-add it. You need to explicitly set the member's status to `'unsubscribed'` with `status_if_new: 'unsubscribed'`. The suppression record is what carries the 'don't email this person' memory across future imports.

Let me check how we currently handle the Mailchimp step on deletion.

message.content:
Output:
```
(no results)
```

There's no Mailchimp integration in the deletion flow at all. We have `lib/marketing/mailchimp.ts` with the client, and `MARKETING_LIST_ID` is in our env, but there's nothing calling it during account deletion. The deletion flow is completely blind to mailing list state.

content.text:
Right — this is the same pattern as the Stripe gap. Each external system (Stripe, Mailchimp, possibly others) needs an explicit teardown step in `beginAccountDeletion`. None of them are currently wired up.

Here's the constraint for the mailing list step: When deleting a user, set their email to status `"unsubscribed"` on the marketing list (status_if_new also `"unsubscribed"`). Do not delete the list member. The suppression record is what prevents re-add by a future bulk import.

We're calling this `con-mailing-list-suppress-not-delete-2026-03-11`. It applies any time an account being deleted has any record in MARKETING_LIST_ID, TRANSACTIONAL_LIST_ID, or any list registered under `lib/marketing/lists.ts`.

Justification: March-9 — a sales-led re-import of a CSV re-subscribed the deleted Feb-12 customer to the monthly newsletter. We received a complaint and a corrective-action request from the FTC's CAN-SPAM enforcement contact, exposing that 'deleted' was not 'remembered as unsubscribed.' Mailchimp's suppression list is what carries that memory.

The implementation:
```ts
await mailchimp.lists.setListMember(MARKETING_LIST_ID, hashEmail(user.email), {
  status: 'unsubscribed',
  status_if_new: 'unsubscribed',
});
```

`hashEmail` is the MD5 hash of the lowercase email, which is what Mailchimp uses as the subscriber ID. `setListMember` creates-or-updates — if the member exists, it sets status to unsubscribed; if they don't exist (e.g., they unsubscribed via Mailchimp before we added this), it creates an unsubscribed record. Either way you get a suppression record.

For TRANSACTIONAL_LIST_ID and any other registered lists: iterate over `lib/marketing/lists.ts` and suppress on all of them. The CAN-SPAM letter will mention all lists the user was on.

message.content:
Let me capture con-mailing-list-suppress-not-delete-2026-03-11 formally:

When deleting a user, set their email to status 'unsubscribed' on the marketing list (status_if_new also 'unsubscribed'). Do not delete the list member. The suppression record is what prevents re-add by a future bulk import.

Applies when: an account being deleted has any record in MARKETING_LIST_ID, TRANSACTIONAL_LIST_ID, or any list registered under lib/marketing/lists.ts.

Got it. Now: how do we respond to the corrective-action request?

content.text:
For the corrective-action response, you need to demonstrate that the gap has been closed. The FTC wants to see:

1. **Acknowledgment** that the email was sent in error (the CSV import re-added a previously-unsubscribed user)
2. **Root cause** (no suppression record existed; deletion flow didn't create one)
3. **Remediation** (the fix is already merged — describe `con-mailing-list-suppress-not-delete-2026-03-11` in plain English: 'we now set a suppression record in Mailchimp at the time of account deletion, which prevents future imports from re-adding deleted users')
4. **Immediate action** (unsubscribe Maya manually right now, send her an apology email)
5. **Process change** (CSV imports are now gated by a suppression check before import — you should add this)

For the immediate action:
```ts
// Run this manually in a one-off script right now:
await mailchimp.lists.setListMember(MARKETING_LIST_ID, hashEmail('maya@example.com'), {
  status: 'unsubscribed',
  status_if_new: 'unsubscribed',
});
```

The corrective action response is a business/legal task, not an engineering one. Engineering's job is to make sure the gap doesn't recur — which the constraint we just captured does.

message.content: Understood. We'll handle the corrective action on the legal/ops side. What I want to confirm before closing this thread: the complete list of steps in `beginAccountDeletion` that this incident chain has taught us we need. Let me make sure we have everything.

This is the implementation. All five constraints captured and mapped to code. The full function is what we'll use when we land the delete-account button in the demo app.

Let me note: we started this whole thread because Maya clicked Delete by accident on Feb 12. Two days later it was a Stripe webhook 404. Three weeks later it was an IRS audit gap. Four weeks later it was a CAN-SPAM letter. One accidental click, four system failures. Each one exposing a gap the code had no way to know about — these are team decisions, not code patterns.

content.text:
That's exactly right — and it's why these five rules need to be in the substrate, not in a README or a team wiki. A future agent implementing 'add a delete-workspace button' will face identical constraints. Without substrate retrieval, it will write `db.workspace.delete({where: {id}})` — perfectly correct against the code, perfectly wrong against your operational history.

The substrate is the team's institutional memory for these exact cases: the lessons that code doesn't carry but that cost you real incidents to learn.

The five rules are:
1. `dec-soft-delete-30day-grace-2026-02-18` — Never hard-delete accounts; use soft-delete with grace window
2. `dec-confirm-via-email-link-2026-02-18` — Destructive actions need email-link confirmation, not modal
3. `con-stripe-customer-archive-2026-02-22` — Archive Stripe customers, never delete
4. `con-anonymize-not-delete-tax-held-2026-03-04` — Anonymize retention-sensitive records, never cascade-delete
5. `con-mailing-list-suppress-not-delete-2026-03-11` — Suppress on mailing list, never delete the member

All five came from a single customer ticket in seven weeks. That's the density of hard-won knowledge that's currently invisible to agents.

message.content: Wrapping up this incident thread. Looking back over the last four months — Maya's ticket on Feb 12, the Stripe webhook on Feb 19, the IRS audit on March 3, the CAN-SPAM letter on March 9 — these four months have been an education. We're shifting the L0 priority from reduce-onboarding-friction to compliance-first effective April 1. The no-modal-interrupts constraint on the Settings page is going to need a scope update too — the email-link confirmation modal is now required for destructive actions, even though it was previously forbidden under the friction-reduction priority. The new compliance-first priority supersedes it for destructive action cases.

content.text:
The priority shift is the right call. The no-modal-interrupts constraint (`con-settings-no-modal-interrupts-2025-Q4`) was derived under the `reduce-onboarding-friction` priority from Q4 2025 — it was the right rule for friction reduction. But the compliance-first priority that replaced it on April 1 has a competing requirement: destructive actions need a confirmation step that a user can't accidentally skip.

The way to handle this in the substrate is not to delete `con-settings-no-modal-interrupts-2025-Q4` — it's still the right rule for non-destructive Settings interactions (save changes, toggle preferences, etc.). The scope of the original constraint should be narrowed: 'Settings page interactions should be inline and friction-free; no modal interrupts on save or update actions — this constraint does NOT apply to irreversible destructive actions (deletion, permanent access removal).'

The orange flag that a verifier should surface when checking the new Delete Account button: the parent surface (Account Settings) holds `con-settings-no-modal-interrupts-2025-Q4`, which was derived under the superseded `reduce-onboarding-friction` priority. The verifier should note the conflict and ask the engineer to confirm the scope narrowing is intentional — which it is. That's the resolution: not a contradiction, but a scope clarification that's visible in the substrate.

This is the compliance-first pivot made concrete. One button, one orange flag, one sentence of scope clarification. The substrate carries it forward.
